speaker: interviewer
text: Number three, interview with Mr. David Shear.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is December 21, 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Shear, would you please describe, how did you get this courage to overcome this horror you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: Being a [? witness. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- unfortunately, it's very-- it is wrong to say that you can get used to as misery occurs. But most of the strength I got was from my youth. It was because-- as I mentioned before, I was practicing-- not just practicing, but we lived on meditation-- transcendental meditation-- to minimize the importance of necessities, to minimize the-- the importance of having things-- have-- I mean, we never complained that we-- because usually when we're young it was not the greatest pleasures that we were involved with. Couldn't expect much. But we had freedom. We did the things that we wanted to do. We didn't want to do anything else. But the courage I really got was the strength of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the religious strength. It is just plain that everything has a-- even if you don't understand it, it has a meaning. It must mean something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's a matter of fact, there was-- while [INAUDIBLE] the subject, it's the same as a-- my [? monitor ?] says, how would you-- can you explain God? How-- how would you-- you're willing to understand God? The more complicated a project is-- I think is, the more difficult is the creator. If somebody creates a fork in the beginning, he was no real big genius. But he's still-- it [? is ?] invention. But how can you explain to-- to understand somebody who creates a-- the television first? It was something that nobody understands. Most other people still don't understand how a computer works. So how would you say you want to be-- understand how the computer-- the man who can-- who invented the computer? Or-- or-- going to the moon-- somebody who-- the Moon landing. Einstein-- Einstein was Einstein. So how can you explain somebody-- how would you understand? So how would you-- can you understand if there is such a thing as somebody who created everything? Einstein, and the Moon, and everything else. So how could you say, well, if I don't understand what God is, there is God. I mean, how would you be-- how could you expect to understand something what's so complicated, and it's so-- it's so-- everything, every move is so complicated. So-- and this is while we're trying to-- the strength what it gave me during the war was-- we were not only the-- the first ones to suffer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe there was a purpose. Maybe there is-- in there is the hope for tomorrow, the hope that things might change, and there might be a future for us. And, of course, it is a-- as I said, religious convictions-- religious strength. It's not only-- it's very difficult to be-- to be logic about things. And it's black and white, or whatever color. And it's not that simple. You've got to have it in your-- you've got to live it through and to see as you go on. And this was the strength that you were.

speaker: interviewer
text: You witnessed a lot of the horror. You practically seen it in your eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not only did I witness, but I-- I had my closest friends-- my closest-- the-- the really people that I thought that I knew that they-- they were real, strong people, people with quality, people which they were-- to me, they were the future of generations. And they were wiped out, without any-- no-- with the blink of an eye, just because somebody didn't like him, or somebody-- or because of this atrocities, what happened, you know. It's very difficult to-- at the time, I never thought that my life-- would-- would-- why it would continue. I said, well, if there would be a day that I'll be able to-- just to breathe as a free person-- I never thought that I am going to continue to be a part of this world of ours. Because nothing meant anything. And as a matter of fact, I would have been a millionaire, if I would have gone back to Poland after the war. Because as I mentioned before, that we had all these rich, wealthy people, which they saw the end. So they came to-- to our house, because they were-- the lumber-- lumber yard. And we dug holes. And we hid so much money, all their-- their fortunes. There was no such thing-- Jews couldn't have their money in the banks, and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was-- we buried over there. And I was the only one who knew where to go and get it back. But nothing meant anything to me. I had no need for-- for anything. Because after I found out that all these-- all these Jewish-- all our lives were destroyed. So I didn't go back. And I didn't want even to face the people there. So I was able to-- as you go on, as they say, [NON-ENGLISH]. You learn, when you're young, when you build in to yourself, this is the strength that you stay with. And this is the thing that you will-- hope will carry you for the rest of your life. In Auschwitz, you were there in 1940-- what? That was winter of 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came down in Auschwitz. When the Russians start to get closer to Poland, they evacuate Auschwitz. And we were put-- we walked-- I think it was '45. It was in January in '45. They made us walk from Auschwitz to a substation. Because they accumulated from all the camps all these people. And they put us on trains. Then we were-- they was-- we were sent to-- we didn't know where we were going. But we ended up in Buchenwald. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And in Buchenwald, of course, we were just kept in Barracks. We were made to do some local work carrying rocks from one place to another, or whatever the roads to build. And we stayed there four weeks in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz, you were for how many months?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, I was [? by ?] about nine months in Auschwitz. And then we went-- they took us to Buchenwald. And after four weeks in Buchenwald, they put us on trains. And we were taken to-- sent to deeper into Germany to new-- a new camp. Because they were-- they built new kinds of factories on the underground. Because the-- the Americans-- they destroyed most of the factories which they had. So in order for them to rebuild, so they put them in the-- on places where there are hills.

speaker: interviewee
text: So on this train, we were blown off the tracks by-- by American planes. And I was shot in my-- I was sitting and having lunch-- having lunch or we were having a bite, whatever. On the-- on the train, I remember sitting with six people. I was talking to them and giving them a pep talk. We felt sort of on a little comfortable side. Because we were traveling. And it was a-- a sunny day. You know, you felt like sort of things are starting to stir a little, or something happened. And all of a sudden, we were blown off the tracks by American planes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of them were dead, except me, that I was-- the rest of them were all-- we jumped out of the track, of course. But nobody survived from these six people. Yes, one survived. But he lost his leg. And I just end up by having a few shrapnels in my arm, which I didn't even notice till I got to-- to this new camp. It was a real muddy-- a new camp, which they built. And when I got there, they told me that this camp is the worst thing you have ever experienced in life. You have to travel for thousands and thousands of, you know, feet down, or-- and you know, it's very unhuman conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- so I devised-- I-- while I was in the hospital, they said, if you can prolong your stay in the hospital as long you can do it, stay in the hospital. If they-- you're better off. Because they're not going to do nothing to you while you're in the hospital. So every time my arm healed, I riped it off apart. I took-- put salt during the night so that the wound shouldn't heal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on account of this arm that was I shot, I stayed in this hospital until I was liberated. I was never-- never experienced any hardship in this camp, except we had no food. We had nothing to live on. But this was the only camp that I knew that is the most-- the most awful place in the world. But I still remained there, until I was liberated by the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hospital? I was there about-- at least for three months or so. After Buchenwald, and then we went there, about three months I was at the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spent--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I spent--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Buchenwald for--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Buchenwald-- and then, from there, we went to this camp. And we were shot. And from there, we stayed-- I stayed in the hospital until April-- April the 11th, when the Americans came. And-- and I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me some more about this period, where you lost everybody in the track-- most of your friends-- on the way to Buchenwald. It must have been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, it was-- all of the sudden, you know, when you try-- you see that your life is finished. Because you see all these people. We were-- actually, when-- when the train stopped, because whatever wagons that are-- they were off the track. But some of them were-- and we jumped out. And we went down on the, you know, on the-- down. We lay down to protect ourselves, because the planes kept on coming down. But after it was over, when they came down, and they saw that it was not a military plane-- they saw it was something else. So they left. And all of a sudden, you know, you-- when you come, and you see a new-- and everything was different. Everything was-- so-- so even misery-- it was a change. And you had to start to find a-- another way of to survive. Like when they operated on my arm, I thought it was the end of it. Because-- so they said, no, don't worry. We are the same-- the doctors-- they were the same people from prisoners. And they say, we cannot do nothing [INAUDIBLE] unless we are told. So, and they said, we are going to fix your arm. And-- and of course, all of a sudden, you see new faces, new people. Like when I was in the hospital, I was mostly with French people. And they all spoke French. And they were more knowledgeable about the-- the war, the way the war was. Because in Auschwitz, we were blind about this-- this old-- what's going on outside. But somehow, the French youngsters-- you know, they-- they got their news. And they knew the movements of the West and the East. And so it was a different experience. But it was the same fight of survival. Because we knew that every day our life is not-- you know, it doesn't mean anything. And the only way we could survive is to stay in this hospital as long as possible. And we didn't know if they are going to let us stay in the hospital. Because usually, when they-- whatever they do, they dispose of the sick people first. But we were lucky in this case. They didn't-- they needed every young person. And the people who run this hospital, they thought that we will be able to be of use to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you well treated in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was very little food. There was very little of anything. And that's why there was no medical care. They didn't know. They were-- that was-- it was the-- almost the end of the war. They had no organization. They were not-- and, of course, they-- most of them, they were-- they didn't care. They just let you, as they say there, there to die. And if you survived, you survived on your own.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was no food. There was no-- any attention, or any human help for-- or any assistance from anything. So as a matter of fact, I came down. When I walked out from this camp, I was exactly 96 pounds. And I was already 25 years old. So I could-- some-- I-- I couldn't believe it, that I was still able to move. And I was still able to-- to function as a human being, you know. But somehow, my mind was still hoping-- hoping to get back to-- to life and to-- never had I any idea. What came to your mind when you survived it all, when you survived the horrors?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- when I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when we got--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a young man, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the night before, which it was April the 10th. And then we heard this cannons, this artillery, and all these planes. So we knew that something is going to happen. So but we had no say in this whole matter. We just could not-- we just lay there and listened. And in the morning, all of a sudden, we hear, you know, there is nobody at the gate. And nobody could believe it. But somehow, we walked out. And we walked to the gate. There was nobody there. And then we went out through the front of the gate. On the road, there was nobody there. Well, a few people. And then we kept on walking. So we walked. And then we saw the Americans-- the tanks and all this. They were moving by.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- by whom were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Americans. The 8th Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- and where were you at the time, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- the name of the city was [GERMAN] Zwieberge. Zwieberge means two hills. It was-- the camp was built between two hills, which they built ammunition factories there. But when the Americans zoomed by, they didn't even-- they didn't know whom they see. Because to them-- to them we were just civilians walking. But they didn't know. They didn't stop, even.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walked to the next little village. And there were already a few people from the camp, and getting bread, and getting some food. So we got a bread, or whatever it was. And then we went back to camp. We had no place to go. Where would I go? So we went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right away, the next day, there are some people came from the city and from the-- some officials, some the militia. And they started to accumulate the dead, which there were-- there were thousands of dead people. So they offered us-- they had the nerve to ask us to bury the-- of course, they said, we're going to give you some food for it. But anyway-- and then they brought in some people to build some mass graves. Because there were so many dead that I've never believe it. Somehow, I don't know where these dead people came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they, scattered?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were scattered. They were all over. They-- it was probably also from the hospital. There were different sections, you know. I was in a section, which were the light-- the mild wounded. But there were other, which there were serious people. And-- and I was told that before the Germans left, they killed a lot of prisoners, those who were not able to walk, those who were not strong enough. They didn't want to leave them behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there for another-- and then, of course, they-- they came. And they organized we should have food and a little medical attention. And a few days later, we just left the camp. And we-- we got together-- a few people, a few of us prisoners from the concentration camp. And we got some transportation to the next-- probably it was an American truck who took us to the next station, where the Americans are-- were stationed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And over there, they were at a grounds, where there were a lot of buildings from the German government. So they told us, go ahead, any place you want to go. Find yourself a place and make it home. So we just got together a few fellows. We went from one house to another. And then you found one place, which we thought we could sleep there. And we started-- we built-- put together. We threw out the furniture. We got some other pieces. And we were right next to the Americans. Because the Americans were downstairs. So we slept over there. We made our place to live there. And for food, we went downstairs. And the Americans gave us all the leftovers-- all the leftovers. They had so much food there. And they gave us enough food to survive, to go on and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the feeling, to eat freely the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, it was so impossible, it just-- your mind doesn't-- you don't even think. Life is so strong that it's just like some-- sometimes, even when-- when a lot of people want to go through a narrow door, so nobody walks out. If everybody's pushing, nobody-- our-- our feelings-- our experience was so full of emotions, was sort of-- right away, all of a sudden, we realized that our lives just had no meaning. What-- now is the other problem. What are you going to do with the rest of your life? Well yet, here I am with some people from Morocco, from France. We don't even speak the same. We don't even think the same. We don't even behave the same. And we knew what happened. We knew that we have lost everything. Although in my case, I hadn't got-- my family wasn't too big at all. But I knew that my past was gone. And I knew it was impossible to-- here I am, in the center in Germany. But it's amazing how strong you are when you are young. We were able to get organized. Of course, life had no meaning. Of course, at the time, they said, you go-- can go in any place and-- and take anything you want. And we went into some buildings, some houses, which there were Nazis-- officers, or generals, or whatever-- all kinds of high-class military men. The only thing we could think is to destroy some mirrors, to break some furniture, to get a little of our [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hatred, express your feelings. I remember I came into a house. There was so much. But I think today, probably the-- some beautiful paintings and all kinds of things. The only thing I walked out with was a little cocoa, which in the kitchen-- that's what I took. And I walked out with it. I did not think of any future. Or let me see, maybe this painting is a-- it is a famous van Gogh or a Picasso. I mean, anything you [INAUDIBLE], you know. Nobody, nothing. Nothing mattered to us. As a matter of fact, it took-- it took us-- took me a long time to-- to realize that money means anything. Of course, in the beginning, you didn't have to have any money. You know, you-- you went into the store. The-- when you walked around with your uniform, you had your-- insignia that you're-- so they-- you were free to take-- to get some-- nothing special or nothing, but food or-- But all of a sudden, after a while, you started to see that life is more than just to survive and to-- especially when you are young, and you have-- life to seize in front of you. And you get sort of back to-- to-- your feelings come back to you and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were pretty unnourished-- 95 pounds is pretty--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was 95 pounds. And I really, in the beginning-- as a matter of fact, I remember getting-- taking sick, ill once in a while. I remember ending up in a hospital to be treated. But it wasn't a-- it wasn't a serious thing. It was sometimes over not to have the right food or not to do the right thing. But we were-- we-- I recuperated very nicely, very easy. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you to tell me a little bit about Goslar after you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Goslar was-- Goslar was we lived there when we got to Goslar. Goslar was-- oh. Goslar--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you get to Goslar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was in '45. '45 we-- when we-- when I came out from this-- liberated in this Zwieberge. Zwieberge was on the other side of the Elbe, which all of a sudden, we were told, there is the Russians are going to [INAUDIBLE]. The Russians are going to take over this part. And it's going to become East and West.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's going to be East and West. So-- and we were told, if we want to stay with the Russians, we can stay. But if we don't want to stay with the Russians, we should-- they'll provide us with transportation and to go across the Elbe to be where the-- with the British, where the British were. So this was the division between East and West Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went on trucks, and they-- whatever we had already-- a little accumulated. And we went across this-- the Elbe. And they put us in a city. They took us to a city. The name was Goslar.
